issues.title: The Macro Crossroads: Four Decades of Economic Cycles and What Comes Next
issues.subtitle: A comprehensive macroeconomic analysis spanning 40 years of economic cycles

themes.name: Macroeconomic Analysis
themes.thesis: The US economy in 2025 sits at a delicate juncture. Having achieved a soft landing from the 2021-2022 inflation surge, policymakers now face constrained policy space, structural headwinds, and technological promise. A 40-year historical perspective reveals limited ammunition for future downturns and the need for prudent portfolio positioning.

issues.summary: This issue provides a comprehensive macroeconomic analysis spanning 40 years of economic cycles (1985-2025). It examines current conditions including GDP growth, inflation, unemployment, Fed policy, and debt levels in historical context. The analysis covers structural shifts including debt accumulation, demographic changes, AI productivity potential, and deglobalization. Investment implications are discussed across base, bull, and bear case scenarios.
keyTakeaways: The Fed has engineered a soft landing, but with limited ammunition for future downturns
Debt-to-GDP at 124% constrains fiscal flexibility in ways not seen since WWII
Structural headwinds (demographics, deglobalization) compete with AI-driven productivity tailwinds
The yield curve's 2022-2024 inversion was a 'false positive'—recession risk remains but is not imminent
Investors should prioritize quality, diversification, and flexibility in this late-cycle environment

issues.title: Semiconductor Infrastructure Deep Dive
issues.subtitle: EUV monopolies, process control leaders, and turnaround opportunities

themes.name: EUV Monopolies
themes.thesis: Extreme ultraviolet lithography is the most critical bottleneck in advanced semiconductor manufacturing. ASML's monopoly position and TSMC's manufacturing dominance create irreplaceable moats.

companies.name: ASML Holding N.V.
companies.bull: Monopolistic position in EUV lithography with no viable competitor for 3nm and below. Order backlog exceeds €36B. High switching costs and decade-long qualification cycles protect market position.
companies.bear: China export restrictions could impact 15-20% of revenue. High R&D intensity requires continuous innovation. Customer concentration risk with TSMC and Samsung.
companies.positionRationale: Core holding in semiconductor infrastructure

companies.name: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing
companies.bull: Dominant foundry with 90%+ market share in advanced nodes. Apple, NVIDIA, AMD dependence creates pricing power. Arizona and Japan fabs diversify geopolitical risk.
companies.bear: Geopolitical risk regarding Taiwan remains the primary concern. Capital intensity requires massive ongoing investment. Margin pressure from customer concentration.
companies.positionRationale: Largest position - the picks and shovels of AI

themes.name: Process Control
themes.thesis: As process nodes shrink, yield management becomes exponentially more critical. KLA's inspection and measurement tools are essential for every major fab.

companies.name: KLA Corporation
companies.bull: Dominant market share in process control with 60%+ in key segments. Recurring service revenue provides stability. Increasing complexity at each node drives tool intensity.
companies.bear: Cyclical exposure to semiconductor capex. China revenue restrictions impacting growth. Valuation premium to semiconductor equipment peers.
companies.positionRationale: Defensive quality in semi equipment

themes.name: Turnaround Plays
themes.thesis: Intel and AMD face different challenges but both offer asymmetric risk/reward. Intel's foundry pivot is high-risk, high-reward. AMD's data center momentum continues.

companies.name: Intel Corporation
companies.bull: Foundry strategy could capture significant TAM if execution succeeds. CHIPS Act funding provides $8.5B in grants. Pat Gelsinger's technical leadership and foundry partnerships.
companies.bear: Massive capex requirements with uncertain returns. Manufacturing delays at 18A node. Market share losses to AMD in data center. Dividend suspended.
companies.positionRationale: Speculative turnaround - size accordingly

companies.name: Advanced Micro Devices
companies.bull: EPYC dominance in data center with 25%+ share. MI300 AI accelerators gaining traction against NVIDIA. Xilinx integration synergies still being realized.
companies.bear: Valuation assumes flawless execution. AI GPU market still NVIDIA-dominated. Gaming and embedded markets remain weak.
companies.positionRationale: Growth at reasonable price vs NVIDIA

issues.summary: This issue examines the critical infrastructure layer of semiconductor manufacturing. EUV lithography remains the ultimate bottleneck, with ASML and TSMC commanding irreplaceable positions. Process control leader KLA offers defensive quality, while Intel presents a high-risk turnaround opportunity and AMD continues its data center momentum.
keyTakeaways: EUV lithography is the most critical chokepoint in advanced semiconductor manufacturing
ASML and TSMC have built irreplaceable moats through decades of R&D and capital investment
Process control intensity increases exponentially at each new node, benefiting KLA
Intel's foundry pivot is a binary outcome - significant upside if execution succeeds
AMD remains the best risk/reward in AI compute outside of NVIDIA

issues.title: The Nuclear Energy Renaissance
issues.subtitle: AI data centers are reigniting nuclear power demand. From uranium miners to next-gen reactors.

themes.name: Uranium & Fuel Supply
themes.thesis: The uranium market is in structural deficit. Primary mine supply covers only ~75% of reactor demand, and the supply gap is widening as new reactors come online globally.

companies.name: Cameco Corporation
companies.bull: World's largest publicly traded uranium company with Westinghouse ownership. Tier-1 assets, structural uranium deficit, 161% 1Y return.
companies.bear: P/E of 121x is extremely elevated. Uranium price cyclicality. Kazatomprom supply risk. Nuclear accident tail risk.
companies.positionRationale: Best-in-class uranium exposure with vertical integration

themes.name: Nuclear Fleet Operators
themes.thesis: Existing nuclear plants are the immediate beneficiaries of AI power demand. Constellation Energy and Vistra own the largest US nuclear fleets and are signing long-term PPAs at premium prices.

companies.name: Constellation Energy Corporation
companies.bull: Largest US nuclear fleet. Microsoft TMI restart deal. Analyst target $398 implies 35%+ upside.
companies.bear: Stock already repriced significantly. TMI restart regulatory risk. EPS expected to decline 10% YoY.
companies.positionRationale: Flagship nuclear fleet operator, direct hyperscaler beneficiary

companies.name: Vistra Corp.
companies.bull: 41 GW diversified capacity. 120% YoY earnings growth expected. Analyst target $239.
companies.bear: P/E of 62x rich for utility. Not a pure nuclear play. Down 22% from highs.
companies.positionRationale: Diversified power play with nuclear upside and retail stability

themes.name: Next-Gen Reactors
themes.thesis: Small modular reactors and advanced fission designs promise to solve nuclear's scale, cost, and timeline challenges. High-risk, high-reward plays on the long-term future.

companies.name: NuScale Power Corporation
companies.bull: Only NRC-certified SMR design. First-mover advantage. Analyst target $34 implies 150%+ upside.
companies.bear: Deep cash burn, class action lawsuit, -77% from highs. Only 330 employees.
companies.positionRationale: Speculative first-mover in SMR technology

companies.name: Oklo Inc.
companies.bull: Advanced fission + fuel recycling. DOE award. Sam Altman backing. Analyst target $116.
companies.bear: Zero revenue, $10.3B valuation, 120 employees. NRC previously denied license. -66% from highs.
companies.positionRationale: Pure venture-stage bet on next-gen nuclear

issues.summary: Nuclear energy is experiencing a generational renaissance driven by AI data center power demand. Hyperscalers have moved from exploration to execution with landmark deals. This issue covers the full nuclear value chain: uranium supply (Cameco), fleet operators (Constellation Energy, Vistra), and next-gen reactors (NuScale, Oklo).
keyTakeaways: AI data center power demand is the primary catalyst — hyperscaler PPAs are validating nuclear as essential infrastructure
Uranium is in structural deficit with no quick supply fix — Cameco is the premier way to play this
Constellation Energy operates the largest US nuclear fleet and is the direct beneficiary of hyperscaler deals
Next-gen reactors (SMR, OKLO) offer asymmetric upside but carry substantial execution and timeline risk
This is a 10-20 year structural cycle — position for duration, not momentum

issues.title: The Nuclear Energy Renaissance
issues.subtitle: AI data centers are reigniting nuclear power demand. From uranium miners to next-gen reactors.
issues.summary: Nuclear energy renaissance driven by AI data center power demand. Covers uranium supply, fleet operators, and next-gen reactors.
keyTakeaways: AI data center power demand is the primary catalyst
Uranium is in structural deficit — Cameco is the premier play
Constellation Energy is the direct beneficiary of hyperscaler deals

issues.title: Nuclear Renaissance — The SMR Commercialization Trade
issues.subtitle: From hyperscaler PPAs to first commercial FIDs: who owns the bottlenecks

themes.name: Cash-Flowing Operators
themes.thesis: The existing US nuclear fleet is the scarcest commodity in the AI buildout. Behind-the-meter PPAs with hyperscalers are repricing 30-year-old assets at premium multiples. CEG and VST own the torque; regulated utilities offer the cleaner safety net.

companies.name: Constellation Energy
companies.bull: Largest US nuclear fleet (~32GW). TMI restart and Calpine acquisition closed Q1'26. Each PPA reset (Microsoft, Meta) steps earnings power higher. Multi-decade contracts at $100+/MWh provide unmatched cash flow visibility among power names.
companies.bear: Already re-rated from ~14x to ~28x fwd. Multiple compression risk if AI capex narrative breaks (DeepSeek-style efficiency shock, hyperscaler cuts). Execution risk on TMI restart timeline.
companies.positionRationale: Best risk-adjusted core position in the theme — real cash flows, real PPAs, real torque

companies.name: Vistra Energy
companies.bull: Comanche Peak + Energy Harbor fleet, Texas-heavy = ERCOT scarcity premium. More merchant exposure than CEG = higher torque to AI capex narrative. Strong FCF supports buybacks.
companies.bear: Higher beta cuts both ways. ERCOT regulatory risk. Coal exposure still a drag on ESG flows.
companies.positionRationale: Higher-torque sibling to CEG; size smaller given beta

companies.name: Dominion Energy
companies.bull: Virginia data center alley = 26% of total load from data centers. 40 GW interconnect pipeline. Regulated returns provide downside protection.
companies.bear: Lower torque than merchant operators. Rate-case risk as data center load shifts cost-of-service. Capex burden compresses near-term FCF.
companies.positionRationale: Defensive nuclear exposure for risk-averse sleeve

themes.name: SMR Developers — Option Value
themes.thesis: First commercial FIDs in 2026-27 will re-rate the entire developer cohort. Pre-revenue today, but the optionality is massive if FOAK delivers on schedule. Size to survive a -50% drawdown.

companies.name: Oklo Inc.
companies.bull: Aurora microreactor; DOE site at INL; 12GW Switch master agreement; Meta 1.2GW deal. Sam Altman halo and Big Tech relationships. First combined license submission expected 2026.
companies.bear: Pre-revenue, pre-NRC license. FOAK cost risk acute. Equity dilution likely before commercial deployment.
companies.positionRationale: Lottery ticket — pure call option on SMR commercialization

companies.name: NuScale Power
companies.bull: Only SMR with US NRC design certification. Doosan and RoPower (Romania) commercial deals. TVA 6GW deployment agreement structure.
companies.bear: UAMPS cancellation in 2023 set the company back materially. Cash burn high. Execution credibility still rebuilding.
companies.positionRationale: Smaller lottery position; certified design is the moat but commercialization unproven

companies.name: GE Vernova
companies.bull: BWRX-300 SMR with TVA Clinch River and OPG Darlington — closest to first commercial deployment. Gas turbine business funds the nuclear ramp (sold out to 2028). Double exposure to grid bottleneck + nuclear renaissance.
companies.bear: Multiple already stretched. Gas turbine cycle peak risk. SMR ramp won't move the needle on revenue until 2028+.
companies.positionRationale: Quality way to play SMRs without pre-revenue risk; cross-references the grid bottleneck theme

themes.name: Fuel Cycle — The Real Bottleneck
themes.thesis: You can announce an SMR; you cannot announce HALEU into existence. The under-discussed angle of the nuclear thesis is the fuel cycle: uranium production, conversion, and especially Western enrichment. This is where supply-demand math is tightest and policy tailwinds strongest.

companies.name: Cameco Corporation
companies.bull: Largest Western uranium producer. 49% Westinghouse stake provides services + fuel + AP1000 exposure. Long-term contracts re-pricing into $80+ term market. Tier-1 jurisdiction (Canada/Australia/Kazakhstan).
companies.bear: Multiple already prices in much of the bull case. Cigar Lake/McArthur River operating risk. Uranium spot drawdowns of -25% are normal even in bull markets.
companies.positionRationale: Cleanest single-name fuel-cycle compounder; pairs with CEG as the core trade

companies.name: Centrus Energy
companies.bull: Only US HALEU producer with operating cascade. Russian LEU Ban Act forced Western enrichment reshoring — Centrus is the policy beneficiary. DOE contracts funding capacity expansion. Pure-play exposure to enrichment chokepoint.
companies.bear: Highly volatile. Single-facility concentration risk (Piketon, OH). Capacity expansion timeline 5+ years. Capital structure thin.
companies.positionRationale: Asymmetric pure-play on the HALEU bottleneck — size small but exposure is unique

companies.name: BWX Technologies
companies.bull: Naval reactor monopoly + HALEU components + medical isotopes. Defense-adjacent earnings smooth out the uranium cycle. Sole-source US Navy reactor provider.
companies.bear: Defense contracting margins capped. Less torque than pure uranium plays. Multiple at high end of historical range.
companies.positionRationale: Lower-volatility complement to CCJ and LEU

companies.name: Sprott Uranium Miners ETF
companies.bull: Diversified exposure to uranium miners and developers. Avoids single-name junior risk while capturing spot price torque. Sprott physical trust dynamics support spot.
companies.bear: ETF structure means -25% drawdowns on any uranium spot correction. Small-cap junior weighting amplifies volatility.
companies.positionRationale: Basket alternative for those uncomfortable single-naming juniors

issues.summary: Three things converged in Q1 2026 that didn't exist 18 months ago: first commercial SMR FIDs landing, hyperscaler PPAs repricing the existing fleet, and a structural uranium deficit. The market is pricing operators as utilities-with-AI-optionality and developers as venture bets — both framings under-discount who actually owns the chokepoints. The under-discussed angle: the fuel cycle, not the reactors, is the real tightest link.
keyTakeaways: First commercial SMR FIDs landing 2026-27 (TVA Clinch River, OPG Darlington) turn 'SMR' from a slide into a capex line item
CEG re-rated from utility multiple (~14x) to ~25-28x fwd on hyperscaler PPAs alone — and earnings power keeps stepping up as contracts reset
Uranium structural deficit: ~140Mlb primary supply vs ~190Mlb reactor demand, term price $80+ and rising
The under-discussed bottleneck is HALEU/enrichment, not reactors — Centrus is the only US producer with an operating cascade
Bipartisan ADVANCE Act + DOE $900M SMR funding + 50GW incremental US data center load by 2030 = rare clean policy alignment
FOAK cost overruns remain the single biggest bear case — Vogtle 3/4 ran $35B over budget; SMR modularity claims unproven until two units deliver on schedule
